results.title: Common Pond Water Problems and Simple Ways to Fix Them - Aquascape, Inc.
results.content:
Green Cloudy Water

Green water is usually caused by suspended algae blooms. UV clarifiers, proper filtration, and beneficial bacteria can help clear the water over time.

Brown or Tea-Colored Water

Brown water often comes from tannins released by leaves, mulch, or organic debris. Removing debris and performing partial water changes typically helps.

Milky or Gray Cloudy Water

This is often caused by suspended sediment, poor filtration, or a temporary bacterial bloom. It’s common after pond cleanouts or heavy rainstorms. Fine filter media, proper pump flow, and patience usually resolve the issue naturally.

## A Healthy Pond Is About Balance [...] Beneficial bacteria products can help naturally break down organic waste and reduce sludge accumulation. Specially formulated sludge-removers work with the pond’s ecosystem instead of harsh chemicals. Routine pond cleanouts and seasonal maintenance also go a long way toward keeping the pond bottom clean and healthy.

## Problem #2: Algae Growth

Algae is probably the most misunderstood pond issue. The truth is, some algae is completely natural and even beneficial in small amounts. It becomes a problem only when growth gets excessive.

Algae thrives when three things are present:

Spring is especially common for algae blooms because pond plants haven’t fully matured yet, leaving excess nutrients available for algae to use.

## pond water problems - algaepond water problems - algae [...] # Common Pond Water Problems and Simple Ways to Fix Them

A beautiful pond should feel relaxing, clear, and full of life. But even the healthiest water gardens can run into occasional water quality issues throughout the season. If your pond water suddenly looks cloudy, develops algae, or starts collecting sludge, don’t panic. These are some of the most common challenges pond owners face, and in most cases, they’re easy to manage with a few simple adjustments.

The key to a healthy pond always comes back to three essentials: proper filtration, good water circulation, and a balanced ecosystem. When those elements are working together, your pond becomes naturally easier to maintain and enjoy.

results.title: The Most Common Pond Problems - and How to Fix Them - Splash Supply Company
results.content:
A well-built Ecosystem Pond will have a biofilter and skimmer installed from Day 1, preventing green-water algae and keeping your water crystal-clear. It will have plant pockets and shelves where you can tuck in pickerel, cattails, waterlilies and all those other string algae-preventing plants, and a sturdy fish-safe EPDM liner protected by heavy-duty underlayment.

See how we build our ponds at Splash

Most pond problems arise when builders – professional or DIY – leave out one of these crucial elements. The best way to avoid these mistakes is to educate yourself before you dig (or hire someone to dig for you.) [...] Prevention: Use Beneficial Bacteria and sludge remover to take out excess nutrients in the water. These nutrients ultimately feed duckweed and watermeal.
 Aeration: A properly designed aeration system uses air bubbles to push the watermeal and duckweed to the edge of the pond. You can then easily remove the plants with a net.
 Chemical Control: Broad spectrum contact herbicides are available and can quickly kill any part of the plant they touch, but you may need to use multiple treatments throughout the season.

Learn more about controlling duckweed and watermeal

## How to Get Rid of Duckweed and Watermeal

Duckweed 1Duckweed and watermeal are the world’s smallest seed-producing plants. While not necessarily harmful to your pond – some people add them as a fish snack – their fast-spreading nature can make them a nuisance for people who want to see their fish.

You have a few options if you want to remove duckweed and watermeal from your pond or lake:
